content.full_text:
City
of
YUBA CITY

September 18, 1991

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804-0814


Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

The Yuba City Attorney has made initial contact with attorney
John Wallace of your office with reference to the following
request.

In my capacity as a current member of the Yuba City Council it
has been recommended by the City Attorney that I seek an advisory
opinion from the Fair Political Practices Commission with reference
to whether or not I am required to disqualify myself on any
decisions that come before the City Council on development projects
in which either the developer is represented before the Council by
engineering company by whom I am employed and/or projects on
which my employer has prepared plans and/or layouts that come
before the Council for approval and in which the developer or the
developers representative makes a personal appearance through a
representative other than a representative of the engineering
company by whom I am employed.

To give you some additional background, Laughlin & Company is
an individually owned business owned by Gary Laughlin locally who
is a registered civil engineer. I am employed by Laughlin &
Company as a building designer and have been so employed by
Laughlin & Company now for several years. My wife is also an
employee of Laughlin & Company. We are both strictly employees of
the engineering company business. We have no interest in the business as
such and it is not a business entity in which either myself or my
wife has a direct or indirect investment of any kind. We are
strictly salaried employees.


1201 CIVIC CENTER BOULEVARD • YUBA CITY, CALIFORNIA 95993 • (916) 741-4601

Page 2


     The engineering company does do a fair amount of development
work for various developers that have occasion to come before the
City Council in connection with development projects from time to
time.     On occasion I in my capacity as an employee of the
engineering company may work on a particular project that comes
before the City Council for approval.  It has been my past practice
that in connection with any projects that come before the City
Council in which I have participated in my capacity as an employee
of the company in terms of working on the plans or the layout for
that project I do in fact disqualify myself and do not vote on the
issue and do not participate in the discussion.

     There are, however, other projects that come before the City
Council in which Laughlin & Company has done the engineering work,
layout and/or plans in which I have not participated in and in which
either a representative of the engineering firm appears on behalf
of the developer or on which the developer personally appears not
represented by a representative of Laughlin & Company that I now
seek guidance and an opinion from your commission as to whether or
not that situation or either of those situations would require me
to disqualify myself as to any decisions on those projects.

     Obviously, these are development projects in which I have no
interest in the real property, the subject of the development, no
interest in the corporation, partnership or entity which is seeking
to develop that project.  In other words as a public official I
have no direct or indirect investment in the business entity that
seeks to develop the particular project before the Council and the
business entity seeking to develop that project is not a source of
income in any fashion to me personally within the meaning of §7103
of the Political Reform Act.

     Notwithstanding and in view of the fact that the issue has
been raised and in order to clear this issue and to provide to me
guidance for future decisions I need to be advised as to whether or
not I am disqualified from any decisions involving development
projects in which a representative of Laughlin & Company appears
before the City Council on that project on the grounds that somehow
the decision will have a financial effect upon my employer, that
presumably being the argument, or any development project in which
Laughlin & Company has prepared the plans without my participation
or involvement whatsoever and in which Laughlin & Company does not
appear before the City Council on the basis that somehow that
constitutes a financial effect upon the employer, i.e. the
engineering firm by whom I am employed.

     It is not my suggestion that these decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon my employer,
to the contrary, Laughlin & Company would have no interest as such

Page 3

in the particular development projects being compensated strictly
on the basis of its hourly charge for time devoted by its personnel
to the project.

If the commission concludes that I am required to disqualify
myself as to any decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect upon my employer, namely the engineering
firm by whom I am employed, I would like some guidance as to what
constitutes a material financial effect on the engineering firm
which employs me.

If there is any further information that you need in order to
provide me an advisory opinion and guidance with reference to this
issue, I would request that you either contact me directly or the
City Attorney who is John Sanbrook with the firm of Rich, Fudge,
Morris & Sanbrook, Inc. at 1129 "D" Street, Marysville,
California 95901, telephone number (916) 742-7371.

Very truly yours,

Dennis Nelson

DN/lev
cc: Jeff Foltz, City Administrator
    John Sanbrook, City Attorney

sections.question: Whether the author, a Yuba City Council member employed by Laughlin & Company (an engineering firm), is required to disqualify himself on decisions involving development projects where: (1) the developer is represented by Laughlin & Company, (2) Laughlin & Company prepared plans/layouts for projects that come before the Council, or (3) the developer appears through a representative other than Laughlin & Company. Additionally, what constitutes a material financial effect on the employer for disqualification purposes under the Political Reform Act.
sections.question_synthetic: A Yuba City Council member employed by an engineering firm seeks guidance on whether he must disqualify himself from voting on development projects based on his employer's involvement in those projects, either through direct representation, plan preparation, or potential financial effects.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter, not an FPPC response. No CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section present. The document contains a detailed QUESTION section embedded within the letter narrative. Document dated September 18, 1991.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: Whether the author, a Yuba City Council member employed by Laughlin & Company (an engineering firm), is required to disqualify himself on decisions involving development projects where: (1) the developer is represented by Laughlin & Company, (2) Laughlin & Company prepared plans/layouts for projects that come before the Council, or (3) the developer appears through a representative other than Laughlin & Company. Additionally, what constitutes a material financial effect on the employer for disqualification purposes under the Political Reform Act.

embedding.summary: A Yuba City Council member employed by Laughlin & Company engineering firm requests an FPPC advisory opinion regarding his disqualification obligations when voting on development projects involving his employer's work or representation.
